overview: The Marathon County Board held an educational meeting featuring presentations on PFAS contamination litigation opportunities and renewable energy regulation. No formal votes were taken as this was an informational session, but the board received detailed briefings on joining multi-district PFAS litigation against chemical manufacturers and learned about county authority regarding large-scale wind and solar projects proposed for the area.
presiding: Not explicitly named in transcript, referred to as 'Mr. Chair'

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call - seven supervisors joined by WebEx, four excused
Public comment period (15 minutes, five speakers signed up)
Presentation on PFAS multi-district litigation opportunity
Presentation on renewable energy regulatory authority
Discussion of county options regarding wind and solar projects

discussions.body: Five residents spoke during public comment. Cindy Nelson from Stratford/Oplane Township reported visiting 200 houses about wind turbine projects and stated no one she spoke with supports the projects. Wendy Rowski from Green Valley urged the board to vote no on advancing the comprehensive plan, objecting to the term 'wind farm' as misleading for industrial energy facilities. Barb Newton and Cindy Hogan from Rib Mountain spoke in support of speed limit reduction and no-passing zone on Double End Road, referencing a petition with 75 signatures. Heidi Pesky from Town of McMillan argued against joint development agreements for wind projects, citing concerns about county liability, road damage costs, and long-term obligations.

Attorney Carrie McDougall from Baron and Budd Law Firm presented via WebEx on the nationwide PFAS litigation. He explained the firm serves as co-lead counsel in the MDL consolidated in Charleston, South Carolina before Judge Richard Gergel. The water provider settlement totaled approximately $12-13 billion from 3M and $3-5 billion from DuPont. McDougall explained Marathon County could pursue claims for airport contamination, landfill issues, and wastewater-related PFAS contamination. The arrangement is a 25% contingency fee with no upfront costs to the county. Vice Chair Dickinson noted the county airport has no known PFAS contamination currently. Supervisor Robinson asked about protecting landowners affected by biosolids spreading, and attorneys clarified claims would be specific to Marathon County's injuries, though the county could distribute settlement proceeds as it chooses.

Attorney Rebecca Roker from Atollis Law, representing Wisconsin Counties Association, presented on county authority over wind and solar projects. She noted the Hub City Wind project from Alliant Energy has no PSC filing yet, and the Marathon Wind project was purchased as part of Hub City. She explained PSC has approved 33 solar projects with zero denials, and projects over 100 megawatts fall under PSC jurisdiction with limited local control. Roker presented four options for counties: do nothing, negotiate a Joint Development Agreement (JDA), intervene in PSC proceedings, or litigate. She strongly recommended JDAs as the most effective tool to protect county interests regarding liability, roads, decommissioning, environmental protections, and emergency response training, noting state law provides minimal protection for local interests.

publicComment: Five speakers addressed the board. Cindy Nelson (Stratford/Oplane Township) opposed wind turbines, reporting 200 households she visited were unanimously against the projects. Wendy Rowski (Green Valley) urged voting no on the comprehensive plan and requested accurate terminology for industrial energy facilities. Barb Newton and Cindy Hogan (Rib Mountain) supported speed reduction and no-passing zone on Double End Road with 75 petition signatures. Heidi Pesky (Town of McMillan) argued against joint development agreements, citing concerns about county liability and long-term obligations.
actionItems: Resolution regarding PFAS litigation participation scheduled for discussion and potential action at next week's meeting
Board members encouraged to contact board chair, administrator, or corporation counsel with questions about PFAS litigation
County to consider options for engaging with proposed wind energy projects before PSC filings occur
Infrastructure committee recommendation on Double End Road speed limit forwarded to full county board for vote
title: Marathon County Board Education Meeting - 3/19/2026 - 3/19/2026 - 3/19/2026
